coredata.dc:title: Microfluidic synthesis of robust carbon dots-functionalized photonic crystals
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ge
Cheng, Rui
Cheng, Hengyang
Yu, Xiao-Qing
Ling, Luting
Wang, Cai-Feng
Chen, Su
coredata.dc:description: Robust hybrid microbeads with multiple optical signals have garnered widespread interests for various applications. However, the inefficient physical combination and the potential toxicity of raw materials (heavy metal-based quantum dots) have limited their sustainable development. Herein, we report a microfluidic strategy to achieve the continuous production of environmentally friendly carbon dots (CDs)-functionalized photonic crystal (PC) microbeads. The efficient mass and heat transfer on microscales and the covalent bonding crosslink between CDs and poly (methyl methacrylate-butyl acrylate-methacrylic acid) (P(MMA-BA-MAA)) colloid particles ensure effective assembly of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) PC microbeads in the microchannel. Benefiting from this, the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads behave stable fluorescence properties, together with highly saturated and angle-independent structural color, which can overcome the optical information confusion and distortion. Meanwhile, the obtained CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads show dual optical signal responsiveness through the variation of fluorescence and structural color under different ionic environment. Based on this, we realize the dual ionic sensing by constructing CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads arrays, which broadens the application of optical hybrid microbeads. In addition, we also prepare robust hybrid film and flexible lamp belt. The obtained robust CDs-functionalized PC hybrid microbeads with various attractive optical properties have a broad prospect in optical display, sensors, biological coding and antireflection coatings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid microbeads
Microfluidics
Carbon dots
Photonic crystals
Ionic sensing

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carboxy-rich P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles
Preparation of dual optical metallic ion sensing array
Preparation of blue LED light belts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of continuous synthesis of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads via microfluidic strategy. (b) Schematic illustration of the formation of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. (c) Schematic diagram (top) and optical images (bottom) of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads showing strong blue fluorescence under UV light and bright green structural color under sunlight. (d) Schematic illustration of the dual ionic sensing of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads array. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. Inset: crystal lattices of the CDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of the pure P(MMA-BA-MAA) (black), CDs (red), and CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads (blue). (d) Micro-IR images corresponding C = O, C-N of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Reflection spectra of P(MMA-BA-MAA) microbeads and CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. (b) PL spectra of CDs solution and CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. Insert: Fluorescent images of CDs solution (left) and CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads (right). (c) The time-resolved PL decay curve of CDs and CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. (d) PL spectra of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads after different storing time. (e) Schematic illustration of the enhancement of reflected light and the reduction of scattered light for CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads under sunlight and luminescence illustration under 365 nm UV light. (f) Microscopic optical images corresponding to schematic illustration (e).

(a) Schematic diagram, (b) Reflection spectra and (c) Microscopic optical images of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads at varying view angles of 0°, 30°, 45° 75°. (d) Microscopic optical images under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) and (e) SEM images under different magnification of a CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads cut with cross section. (f) Confocal image and (g) Metallographic microscope image of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads.

Application of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads in visual fluorescence and structural color dual ionic sensing. (a) Optical images of adding 9 metallic ions (10-3 M in water, pH = 7) on the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads and a blank sample. (b) Reflection spectra corresponding to the above optical images (Inset: redshift histogram of the reflection spectrum). (c) PL spectra of adding 9 metallic ions (10-3 M in water, pH = 7) on the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads and a blank sample (Inset: quench histogram of the PL spectrum). (d and e) Patterns of reflection spectra and PL spectra of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads array in presence of 9 metallic ion sensing (Mg2+, Li+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Co2+) with concentration (C1-C6: 10-2-10-7 M).

Schematic illustration and microscopic optical images of (a) P(MMA-BA-MAA) film and (b) CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) film. SEM images of (c) P(MMA-BA-MAA) film and (d) CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) film. (e) Schematic illustration of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads applying in the light belt. (f-h) Digital photos of light belt when the switch is on: (f) wound on stainless steel rod, (g) used for patterning (heart patterning), (h) used in wearable fluorescent bracelets.

head.ce:title: Microfluidic synthesis of robust carbon dots-functionalized photonic crystals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering
College of Chemical Engineering, and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study reported a microfluidic strategy to achieve the continuous production of environmentally friendly dual optical signal carbon dots (CDs)-functionalized photonic crystal (PC) microbeads with stable fluorescence as well as highly saturated and angle-independent structural color.

Robust hybrid microbeads with multiple optical signals have garnered widespread interests for various applications. However, the inefficient physical combination and the potential toxicity of raw materials (heavy metal-based quantum dots) have limited their sustainable development. Herein, we report a microfluidic strategy to achieve the continuous production of environmentally friendly carbon dots (CDs)-functionalized photonic crystal (PC) microbeads. The efficient mass and heat transfer on microscales and the covalent bonding crosslink between CDs and poly (methyl methacrylate-butyl acrylate-methacrylic acid) (P(MMA-BA-MAA)) colloid particles ensure effective assembly of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) PC microbeads in the microchannel. Benefiting from this, the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads behave stable fluorescence properties, together with highly saturated and angle-independent structural color, which can overcome the optical information confusion and distortion. Meanwhile, the obtained CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads show dual optical signal responsiveness through the variation of fluorescence and structural color under different ionic environment. Based on this, we realize the dual ionic sensing by constructing CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads arrays, which broadens the application of optical hybrid microbeads. In addition, we also prepare robust hybrid film and flexible lamp belt. The obtained robust CDs-functionalized PC hybrid microbeads with various attractive optical properties have a broad prospect in optical display, sensors, biological coding and antireflection coatings.

ce:section.ce:para: Hybrid microbeads with collective properties, large surface-volume ratio and efficient fixation of the capture probe have a growing explosion in various applications, such as sensors [1,2], biomaterials [3], anti-counterfeiting [4], pattern displays [5,6], biological detections [7,8] and tumor diagnosis [9,10]. In this regard, quantum dots (QDs) possess attractive optical properties including ultra-fast optical nonlinear response and photoluminescence (PL) due to their small size, making them a new generation of luminescent materials, which have been widely applied in various fields [11–15]. While photonic crystals (PCs) possess unique optical diffraction and photonic band gap characteristics due to the periodic change of refractive index, holding great promise for the preparation of various optical devices [16–20]. In particular, the construction of hybrid microbeads with collective optical properties by QDs and PCs is of special interest [21,22]. Since Lodahl et al. [23] studied the spontaneous emission dynamics of QDs controlled by PCs in 2004, various methods, including layer-by-layer self-assembly [24], infiltration [25] and swelling entrapping [26], have been proposed to construct optical hybrid microbeads. However, most of previously reported optical hybrid microbeads were prepared from physical mixing PCs and QDs by non-covalent bond forces in a three-neck flask. These methods show drawbacks of poor orienting ability, low structured controllability and assembly efficiency, time-consuming, and cumbersome steps. To this end, various efforts have been made to improve the aforementioned problems. Zhang et al. prepared functional QDs microbeads by encapsulation and carboxylation of CdSSe/ZnS QDs on poly (styrene-co-maleic anhydride) using a single-pot method [27]. Liu et al. prepared uniform QD-encoded microbeads by embedding core–shell CdSe/ZnS QDs into polyethylene glycol diacrylate through in situ photopolymerization in microfluidic device [28]. Li et al. prepared quantum-dot-tagged photonic crystal beads via self-assembly of CdSe/ZnS quantum dot-embedded polystyrene nanospheres into photonic crystal beads through a microfluidic device [29]. Our group also carried out relevant works, assembling QDs on PCs to improve the optical performance of hybrid microbeads [30–32]. Despite great advances in this field, the previously reported hybrid microbeads commonly utilize QDs containing harmful heavy metals, which limit the extensive application of hybrid optical microbeads. In addition, the typical building blocks of PCs, such as monodisperse polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), polystyrene (PS) emulsions, and silica microspheres, behave high glass transition temperature and scarce specific functional groups, which show weak interaction between QDs and PCs. This motivated us to explore an alternative assembly strategy to prepare robust hybrid microbeads with excellent optical properties from heavy metal-free, environmentally friendly and superior-performance materials.

Microfluidic technology was originally introduced by Manz for the miniaturized total chemical analysis system in 1990s [33]. The advantages of effective mass and heat transfer, together with high controllability have made microfluidic technology as advanced assembling methods for preparing functional materials toward various fields [34–36]. The multiphase laminar flows and state of each phase can be precisely adjusted in microchannel, which can effectively control the assembling process of droplets [37–39]. Among them, microfluidic droplet technology, which produces droplets with small volume for rapid mixing and stable reaction conditions, has become an ideal platform for chemical reaction [40,41].

In this work, we demonstrate the continuous production of environmentally friendly carbon dots (CDs)-functionalized PC microbeads by amidation of heavy metal-free, amine-rich CDs and carboxyl-rich PC colloidal particles in microchannels. The work includes the following advantages: Firstly, the efficient mass and heat transfer on microscales and the covalent bonding interaction between CDs and poly (methyl methacrylate-butyl acrylate-methacrylic acid) (P(MMA-BA-MAA)) colloid particles ensure effective assembly of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) PC microbeads in the microchannel. Secondly, the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads with excellent collective optical properties circumvent problems associated with the fluorescence attenuation or quenching of CDs caused by aggregation, and poor structural color and angle dependence of PCs, which can suppress optical information confusion and distortion. Furthermore, the obtained hybrid microbeads show dual optical signal responsiveness through the visualization of fluorescence and structural color, broadening the application of optical hybrid microbeads. Specific examples show CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrids with above characteristics can be useful for dual ionic sensing by constructing hybrid microbeads arrays, robust PC films and fluorescent lamp belts. With a number of merits, these CDs-functionalized PC microbeads possess great prospects in applications of optical displays, sensors, biocoding, anti-reflection coating and biological probes. This work is of great significance for controllable integration of nano-/micro-structural units to form new multi-functional structural materials.

Citric acid anhydrous (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), potassium persulfate (KPS), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin biochemical technology Co., Ltd. Analytical reagent grade of methyl methacrylate (MMA), butyl acrylate (BA), methacrylic acid (MAA), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), emulsifier (CO897), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Magnesium(II) nitrate hexahydrate, lithium(I) chloride, ferric(III) chloride, lead(II) nitrate, nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate, copper(II) chloride, cadmium(II) chloride, zinc(II) chloride and cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate were all provided by Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water with resistivity of 18 M Ω/cm was used in all experiments. All of the chemicals were used directly without further purification.

Amine-rich CDs were synthesized using CA and EDA as precursors via a facile one-step solvothermal synthesis method according to a previously reported work [42] with proper modification. Typically, CA (1 g), EDA (2 g) and water (10 mL) were put into a stainless steel autoclave for thermal reaction at 200 °C for 3 h. The product was then dissolved in water and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained supernatant was subsequently filtered by syringe filter membrane (0.22 μm), and the filtrate was collected and dried at 60 °C for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carboxy-rich P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles
ce:section.ce:para: Carboxy-rich ploy(methyl methacrylate-butyl acrylate-methacrylic acid) (P(MMA-BA-MAA)) colloidal particles were synthesized by seed emulsion polymerization. Briefly, 0.13 g emulsifier, 0.02 g SDS, 0.3 g NaHCO3 and 100 mL H2O as base solution were put into a 250 mL four-necked round flask. The mixture was heated to 80 °C, and then the seed solution containing 3 g BA, 8 g MMA, 2 g MAA, 0.16 g emulsifier, 0.02 g SDS and 5 g H2O was added. Subsequently, an initiator was prepared by 0.32 g KPS and 15 g H2O, and 5 mL of initiator was added into the above solution to initiate polymerization (speed 15 mL/h). Then the pre-emulsion (3.5 g BA, 30 g MMA, 2 g MAA, 0.3 g CO897, 0.08 g SDS and 10 g H2O) was added after 1 h. And the remaining initiator (~10 mL, speed 10 mL/h) was injected when it heated up to 85 °C. The whole reaction process took about 6 h with inert gas under sustained stirring. A small amount of aggregation was removed using nylon net (200 mesh) after the reaction, and the filtrate was centrifuged three times at a speed of 12000 rpm for 30 min. Carboxyl-rich P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles were collected. The activated P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloid particles were obtained as follows: 2.6 g of as-prepared P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloid particles were dispersed in 40 mL ultrapure water. Subsequently, EDC and NHS were added (EDC: 0.05 g dissolved in 1 mL ultrapure water, NHS: 0.025 g dissolved in 1 mL ultrapure water) followed by stirring for 30 min. 30 wt% activated P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles were used after centrifugation and dissolution.

In order to obtain CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads, the microfluidic platform consisting of 27G needle, PDMS microtube and T-type chip was designed, where 30 wt% P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles and 1 wt% CDs solution (flow rate 0.15 mL/h) as discontinuous phase, and methylsilicon oil as continuous phase. Driven by the syringe pump, the discontinuous phase was cut into monodisperse droplets by the fast flow rate of continuous phase. In a microchannel placed on a 60 °C heating platform, an amidation reaction occurred between activated carboxyl-rich P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloid particles and amine-rich CDs in the presence of EDC and NHS, to form CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particle microbeads, which were collected in PE collection cups. After being placed in a 45 °C oven for 24 h, CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles self-assembled to form CDs-functionalized PC microbeads.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dual optical metallic ion sensing array
ce:section.ce:para: Templates of highly ordered circular holes array panel with uniform diameter of 600 μm, spacing distance of 800 μm and depth of 500 μm were fabricated through carving 2 mm thick PMMA substrates. CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads (about 390 μm in diameter) dispersed in methyl silicone oil were injected into the surface of PMMA substrate with a 50 mL syringe. The PMMA substrate was oscillated horizontally so that each hole on the substrate could be filled with optical hybrid microbeads. Using oil absorbent paper to gently scrape off the excess microbeads and remove the excess methyl silicone oil, the array filled with CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads was formed with green in sunlight and blue under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. Nine kinds of metallic ions (Mg2+, Li+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Co2+) with different concentrations (10-2-10-7 M) were implanted into holes containing CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads with 50 μL micropipette to obtain dual metallic ion sensing array with structural color and fluorescence responses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of blue LED light belts
ce:section.ce:para: The blue LED light belt was prepared based on copper wire with UV led chips emitted at 390 nm as the substrate. When preparing blue LED light belt, first of all, 1 g of silicone (LED packaging material) was prepared by fully mixing 0.5 g of OE-6550A with 0.5 g of OE-6550B. Secondly, an appropriate amount of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads were added to the silicone and stirred evenly. Then, the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads silicone mixture was transferred to a 5 mL syringe after removing bubbles, connecting the syringe to a precision dispensing machine. The CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads wrapped silicone were dotted on the substrate with UV chip through the dispensing machine. Finally, the finished light belt was placed in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 2 h to cure the silicone, and LED light belt emitting blue fluorescence was obtained.

Robust PC films were prepared with 30 wt% CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) emulsion as raw materials by wire rod scraping method. The 30 wt% CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) emulsion was obtained by fully reacting the activated P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles and CDs through the microfluidic device in the absence of methyl silicone oil. CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) emulsion was dropped on a black substrate, and was scraped out by a wire rod with 30 mm depth. The as-synthesized CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) films presenting bright green in sunlight were obtained. For comparison, P(MMA-BA-MAA) PCs films were prepared by the above methods.

The microstructure and surface morphology of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads were observed by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100). The crystallinity of samples was characterized using a Bruker-AXS D8 ADVANCE advanced X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The chemical structure of samples was analyzed by Thermo's Nicolet-6700 Fourier infrared spectrometer (FT-IR). The micro-IR images of the groups of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads were measured by Thermo Scientific Nicolet iN10 infrared microscope equipped with liquid nitrogen refrigerated MCT detector. The structural color of samples was observed using a stereo microscope (SMZ800) manufactured and their reflection spectra were measured by an optical fiber spectrometer (USB4000) manufactured by Ocean Optics. The PL spectra and time-resolved PL decay curves of samples were measured by FLS 980-STM fluorescence spectrometer. Fluorescence images of samples were obtained using a Zeiss AXIO5 imager optical microscope under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and the Leica TCS/SP2 confocal microscope system. Optical images of samples were taken with SZM45 stereo microscope and color CCD camera. The micromorphology of samples was observed by S-4800 cold field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of CDs were measured on a PerkinElmer Lambda 900 UV–vis spectrometer.

The continuous synthesis of robust CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads starts from preparation of CDs via a facile solvothermal synthesis (Fig. S1a) and P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles via seed emulsion polymerization (Fig. S1b). The CDs are amine-rich, environmentally friendly and cost-effective, together with good PL properties with quantum yield of 82% and half-peak width of 68 nm. There is no significant shift in peak position when the excitation wavelength ranges from 350 to 420 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength is 390 nm, and the optimal emission peak is located at 450 nm, corresponding to blue emission (Fig. S2a). A strong UV absorption peak near 400 nm is observed, and PL emission of CDs is centered at 450 nm and excited at 350 nm as shown in Fig. S2b. The carboxyl-rich P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles possess good monodispersability and the average size is 257 nm, as shown in Fig. S2c. The reflection spectrum shows that P(MMA-BA-MAA) PCs have green structural color (Fig. S2d). Subsequently, the CDs solution containing amino group and colloidal particles containing carboxyl group as building blocks are efficiently assembled into CDs-functionalized PC optical hybrid microbeads through an accessible and continuous microfluidic assembly strategy (Fig. 1 a, Fig. S3). Initially, we investigated the effect of CDs concentration on the CDs-functionalized PC microbeads, as shown in Fig. S4. It was found that the structural color of the hybrid microbeads became dim when CDs concentration was larger than 1 wt%. While lower CDs concentration (<1 wt%) led to weaker fluorescence intensity. By comprehensively considering the structural color and fluorescence characteristics of the hybrid microbeads, 1 wt% CDs concentration was selected in all the following experiments. The assembly is efficient because of amidation between the amino group and the carboxyl group on the building blocks of CDs and PCs, respectively (Fig. 1b). The synthesized CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads show dual optical signals with bright green structural color under sunlight and strong blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1c). Based on the collective optical properties, the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads were applied in versatile ionic sensing with dual optical performance of visual fluorescence and structural color (Fig. 1d). Since the loading of CDs onto the PCs not only overcomes the fluorescence attenuation and quenching of CDs, but also effectively prevents the PCs from cracking film-forming and chromatic aberration, the applications of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) for robust PC films and wearable light belts are available.

The microstructure of the as-prepared CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads was characterized by TEM. As seen in Fig. 2 a, P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles loaded with CDs maintain a clear core–shell spherical structure. The high-resolution TEM of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles reflects that a large number of CDs with an average particle size of 3.73 nm are uniformly loaded on the surface of P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles without obvious aggregation (Fig. 2b, Fig. S5a), indicating that CDs have been successfully loaded on the P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles. The inset of Fig. 2b shows the lattice structure of CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, which can be specified as the d spacings of the graphite (002) planes [43,44]. In addition, XRD curve of pure CDs (Fig. S5b) has an apparent peak at around 2θ = 24°, which is the characteristic (002) diffraction peak corresponding to the graphene structure with crystallinity [45,46]. We further verified the composition of the hybrid microbeads by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). As seen from the black curve in the FT-IR spectra, the absorption peaks noticed as 3600–3200 cm−1, 1720 cm−1, 1320–1210 cm−1 are ascribed to O–H stretching vibration, C = O asymmetric stretching vibration, and C-O stretching, respectively, which validate that the P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles are rich in carboxyl groups. The FT-IR spectra of CDs (red curve) exhibit characteristic absorption band at 3500–3100 cm−1, 1640–1560 cm−1 and 1350–1000 cm−1, corresponding to N–H stretching vibrations, N–H bending vibration and C-N stretching vibration of amine, respectively. The FT-IR spectra of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads (blue curve) presents new absorption peaks at 1652 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 compared to P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles, which are ascribed to typical C = O stretching vibration and N–H bending vibration peaks of amide, respectively. Furthermore, the micro-IR images of the C = O, C-N groups intensity corresponding to the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads further confirm the formation of amide bond (Fig. 2d). All these IR evidences prove that CDs are successfully grafted on the surface of P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles by amidation reaction. Another evidence is proved by the fact that the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles still possess a bright blue fluorescence at the bottom of the entire centrifuge tube after centrifuged at 12000 rpm high speed for 15 min. This feature also indicates that the formation of CDs-loaded P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles circumvents the fluorescence quenching induced by aggregations of CDs in solid state (Fig. S6).

The CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads possess excellent optical properties. We investigated the properties of structural color, fluorescence intensity, time-resolved PL decay and fluorescence stability of the as-prepared microbeads (Fig. 3 ). As shown in Fig. 3a, the reflection spectral intensity of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads is significantly enhanced. And the reflectance peak position shows a slight redshift (~3 nm) compared with that of P(MMA-BA-MAA) control sample. According to the reported literature [6], this can be attributed to the loading of black CDs particles, which can reduce optical scattering and increase reflection, leading to a brighter and more saturated structural color. Fig. 3b demonstrates the PL spectra of CDs solution and CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. Usually, CDs solution possesses excellent fluorescence property. While for solid CDs powders, the aggregation will result in the quenching of the CDs’ fluorescence [47,48]. Interestingly, in our case, the PL intensity and PL peak position of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads keep almost the same with that of CDs solution. The results confirm that loading of CDs on P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles can effectively avoid aggregation and fluorescence quenching of CDs. Generally, there are mainly two ways of time-resolved PL decay of CDs at present, one is multi-exponential decay (multiple relaxation attenuation) [49,50], the other is mono-exponential decay [51,52]. The as-prepared CDs in this work exhibit mono-exponential decay. As seen from the time-resolved PL decay curve in Fig. 3c, the PL lifetime of CDs and CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads is 8.99 ns and 8.90 ns, respectively, which reveals that the CDs loading on PCs can maintain monoexponential decay characteristic, and there is no non-radiation emission channel in the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. Notably, the PL strength of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads do not decrease significantly after being placed for even one month, manifesting good fluorescence stability of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads (Fig. 3d). Fig. 3e reveals the reason for the more distinct structural colors and bright blue fluorescence of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. CDs loaded onto the surface of P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles can enhance the diffraction color by increasing reflection and reducing scattering. The microscopic optical images of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads corresponding to Fig. 3e display more distinct structural colors and bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 3f).

PCs commonly present different structural colors when viewed from different angles, which limits their application in some optical materials and devices desiring wide viewing-angles [53,54]. The as-prepared CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads via microfluidic strategy show angle-independent structural color. This can be ascribed to the spherically symmetric structure of microbeads, where the photonic band gaps and reflection wavelengths are independent of the rotation under illumination of the surface at a fixed incident angle of the light [55,56], as schematically shown in Fig. 4 a. As shown in Fig. 4b, the reflection spectra of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads observed from different angles do not significantly move and all the reflected spectra are centered at about 530 nm with the same position, indicating that the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads have an angle-independent photonic band gap. Microscopic optical images of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads under different angles are presented in Fig. 4c, all showing green structural color. In addition, it also presents good structural color for the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads, not only on their surface, but also in their cross section (Fig. 4d, left). Both surface and cross section of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbead emit bright blue fluorescence under the irradiation of UV light (Fig. 4d, right). The phenomena indicate that CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads are highly ordered from the surface to the interior. To verify the above conjecture, we studied the surface and internal microstructure of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads. Fig. 4e shows the SEM images of surface and cross section of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads at different magnification. They are still arranged in a hexagonal dense accumulation structure with orderly growth process both on the surface and inside. And the surface and cross section of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads at high magnification are quasiorder packing, which may reduce the iridescence and produces saturated structural color [57]. Confocal image (Fig. 4f) and metallographic microscope image (Fig. 4g) both show that the prepared CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads possess bright blue fluorescence. These results demonstrate that high-quality optical hybrid microbeads can be assembled via microfluidic droplet technique.

Interestingly, the structural color and fluorescence intensity of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads respond to different metallic ions (Fig. 5 a, Fig. S7). We investigated the fluorescence and structural color changes of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads in different metallic ions. As shown in Fig. 5b, the reflection spectra of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads in different metallic ions show different degrees of redshift. Especially in the presence of Fe3+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Cd2+ metallic ions, the structural color of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads exists obvious redshift. The histogram inserted in Fig. 5b more intuitively shows the degree of redshift of hybrid microbeads in the presence of different metallic ions. This phenomenon may be attributed to the different metallic ions changing the photonic band gap of the PCs, resulting in the reflection spectra redshift. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads changes in the presence of different metallic ions (Fig. 5c). In particular, in the presence of Fe3+ metallic ion, the fluorescence of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads is almost quenched, and Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Co2+ metallic ions reduce the fluorescence intensity, which can be clearly observed from inserted in Fig. 5c. Based on the change of the structural color and fluorescence of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads with different metallic ions, we carried out the response of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads to 9 metallic ions with different concentrations (10-2-10-7 M). The reflection and fluorescence spectra of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads array in 9 metallic ions with different concentrations (10-2-10-7 M) on CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads array are shown in Fig. 5d and 5e. As shown in the spectra, the fluorescence intensity and structural color of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads are responsive even when the concentration was as low as 10-7 M. The microscopic optical images and microscopic fluorescence images corresponding to the spectrogram of Fig. 5d and 5e are shown in Fig. S8, which can more clearly present the response of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads to metallic ions at different concentrations. CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads array can simultaneously extract the corresponding structural colors and fluorescence optical signals of different metallic ions, thus ensuring the same test environment and saving the measurement time. This phenomenon reveals that CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads array can convert the collection of reflection and fluorescence spectra information into the extraction of optical signals, which can be applied to the visual detection of ionic sensing with dual response of structural color and fluorescence.

In addition, we found that the film prepared by CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles not only has more vivid structural color, but also can reduce the cracking. The P(MMA-BA-MAA) film and CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) film were prepared by wire rod scraping. As shown in Fig. 6 a and 6b, the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) film has more bright structural color and less cracking compared with P(MMA-BA-MAA) film, which is further proved by microscopic optical images. The brighter structural color of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) film is mainly attributed to the fact that CDs loading can finally enhance the diffraction color by increasing reflection and reducing scattering. By comparing the SEM images (Fig. 6c and 6d) of the original P(MMA-BA-MAA) and the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles, we can clearly observe that the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles have less cracking, which may be due to the fact that CDs loading on P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles through covalent bond enhance the interaction between microspheres. In addition, the as-prepared CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) film not only has bright structural color and less cracking, but also maintains bright blue fluorescence (Fig. S9).

Based on the blue fluorescence properties of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads, we also applied the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads in light belts, as shown in Fig. 6e. The as-prepared light belt converted the short-wavelength light of the CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbead into long-wavelength light through a UV chip emitting at 390 nm. In brief, the as-prepared hybrid microbeads were fixed on a UV led chip emitting 390 nm through silicone. The light belt emits bright blue fluorescence when the switch is turned on, as shown in Fig. S10a. Fig. 6f-6 h are digital photos of light belt when the switch is turned on. As can be seen from Fig. 6f, CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads fixed on the substrate maintain good fluorescence even if they are wound on the stainless steel rod, indicating that the fluorescent light belt prepared has great bendability. In addition, we made a heart-shaped fluorescent pattern with the as-prepared fluorescent light belt (Fig. 6g). Due to the raw materials used in the preparation of CDs-P(MMA-BA-MAA) hybrid microbeads are low toxic and harmless, the as-prepared fluorescent light belt can be used in wearable bracelets (Fig. 6h). When the switch is close, the digital photos of light belt under sunlight corresponding to the above fluorescent picture are shown in Fig. S10b-10d. The optical hybrid microbeads prepared by microfluidic technique can realize the optical signal collection of structural color and fluorescence properties, which broadens the application of fluorescent hybrid microbeads. Although some progress has been made, efforts are still needed to explore more possibilities for optical hybrid microbeads. This prompts us to explore multi-functional and multi-color environmentally friendly optical hybrid microbeads to meet the needs of extensive practical applications.

In summary, we have successfully established a continuous strategy to construct high-quality optical hybrid microbeads, enabling heavy metal-free and environmentally friendly CDs to be quickly and efficiently assembled into P(MMA-BA-MAA) colloidal particles. By simply changing the velocity and interfacial tension of binary immiscible phase, uniform spherical microspheres can be obtained. Encouragingly, the synthesized hybrid microbeads not only have stable strong fluorescence characteristics, but also have brighter angle-independent structural color. With dual optical response to metallic ions, we prepared metallic ions sensing with fluorescence and structural color dual response. Based on its excellent optical properties, we also prepared robust hybrid film and flexible lamp belt. It is reasonable to speculate that the directional assembly of CDs and PCs using microfluidic technique can not only be used to produce a variety of novel hybrid microbeads, but also be of great significance for packaging other multifunctional nanocomposites. This work is of great significance for controllable integration of nano-/micro-structural units to form new multi-functional structural materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21736006, 21706122, 21978132), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFB0401700 and 2018YFC1602800), Fund of State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (ZK201704 and ZK201716), and Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.126539.

J. Hou, M. Li, Y. Song, Patterned colloidal photonic crystals, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 57 (2018) 2544-2553.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ensembles of photonic beads: optical properties and enhanced light—matter interactions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Aravindakshan, E. Eftekhari, S.H. Tan, X. Li, J. St John, N.T. Nguyen, H. Zhao, D. Zhao, Q. Li, Ensembles of photonic beads: optical properties and enhanced light—matter interactions, Adv. Opt. Mater. 8 (2020) 1901537.
